rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DUANE LEE FRISBIE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] Reed v. King (1983) 145 Cal. App. 3d 261 considered whether a property's reputation and history would qualify as "material," requiring disclosure. In Reed v. King, the court noted, "Whether information 'is of sufficient materiality to affect the value or desirability of the property ... depends on the facts of the particular case.' [citation.] Materiality 'is a question of law, and is part of the concept of right to rely or justifiable reliance.' [citation.] [] Three considerations bear on this legal conclusion: the gravity of the harm inflicted by nondisclosure; the fairness of imposing a duty of discovery on the buyer as an alternative to compelling disclosure, and the impact on the stability of contracts if rescission is permitted." (Id. at 265-266.) In Assilzadeh v. California Federal Bank (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 399, 410, the court focused on whether facts would have "a significant and measurable effect on market value." In Assilzadeh, the court found that disclosure of a construction defect lawsuit's existence and settlement was adequate to satisfy disclosure obligations, without requiring disclosure of detailed settlement terms. Litigation or disputes related to tenancy in common ownership that materially impact the property's value or desirability would likely require disclosure as a material fact, too. (See, e.g., Calemine v. Samuelson (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 153, While the existence of previous water intrusion at the condominium property was disclosed, the existence of two settled lawsuits relating to that water intrusion was not and was a material fact within the duty of disclosure.) The terms of confidential settlement agreements may be disclosed when the interest in disclosure of the terms is shown to outweigh the confidentiality concerns. (see, e.g., Assilzadeh v. California Federal Bank (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 399; see also, Home Ins. Co. v. Superior Court (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 1286, 1291, "[Non-settling excess insurer's] right to discover the amounts of the [other insurers'] settlements and to have those amounts allocated cannot be seriously disputed.") The courts have not, however, endorsed wholesale publication of confidential settlement agreements where tailored disclosure would balance the competing interests. In Assilzadeh, the court found that disclosure of a construction defect lawsuit's existence and settlement was adequate to satisfy disclosure obligations, without requiring disclosure of detailed settlement terms. In Home Insurance Co. v. Superior Court, the appellate court noted that even when disclosure was ordered, the court "will, of course" make any disclosure order subject to a comprehensive protective order (46 Cal.App.4th at 1293 fn. 6).

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DUANE LEE FRISBIE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For In Camera Review Of Confidential Documents And For Order Authorizing Limited Disclosure To Prospective Real Property Buyers And To The Probate Referee
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Executor Bushra Khan's Motion for In-Camera Submission of Confidential Documents and Authority for Limited Disclosure to Prospective Real-Property Buyers and to the Probate Referee ("Motion"). The court has reviewed the confidential settlement document submitted for in-camera review, in consideration of the Motion's request for an order authorizing disclosure of the submitted confidential settlement agreement to prospective buyers of the estate real property, and to the probate referee as part of a reassessment of the estate real property's value (see, Evid. C. sec. 915). The other party to the confidential settlement agreement filed a timely Response to the Motion. The Motion is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART, as follows. The confidential Settlement Agreement and Mutual Release and attached exhibits ("Settlement Agreement") submitted for in-camera review was made with respect to a civil complaint Decedent Duane L. Frisbie brought against John and Teresa Votruba in this court in 2017, case number CGC-17-560168. On order of the court, the parties proceeded to binding arbitration before Hon. Ina L. Gyemant (Ret.). Exhibit 1 to the settlement agreement is Judge Gyemant's Final Award on Phase 1 Issues, deciding the parties' claims against each other in the civil litigation regarding the parties' tenancy-in-common, and entering orders in her role as arbitrator. Exhibit 2 to the settlement is the April 26, 2004 Tenancy-In-Common Ownership Agreement as amended on December 4 and December 27, 2004, certain terms of which were at issue in the litigation and before the arbitrator. This Tenancy-In-Common Ownership Agreement document is also filed as an attachment to the Response to the Motion. In the context of residential property transfers, buyers possess fundamental rights to material information affecting property value under Civil Code sections 1102-1103.8, which establish mandatory disclosure obligations that courts must enforce regardless of private confidentiality agreements. These comprehensive disclosure requirements focus on physical property conditions, environmental hazards, and material defects, but also include the Seller's disclosure of whether there are "CC&R or other deed restrictions or obligations" and "[a]ny lawsuits by or against the Seller threatening to or affecting this real property [.]" (Civ. C. sec. 1102.6, statutory form at subs. C(12) and (16)). The statutory disclosure obligations cannot be circumvented by private confidentiality arrangements (see, e.g., Realmuto v. Gagnard (2003) 110 Cal. App. 4th 193). These statutory disclosure requirements do not specifically address ownership structures, such as tenancy in common. However, in addition to the statutory disclosure requirements under Civil Code sections 1102-1103.8, agents in residential property transfers have a statutory duty under Civil Code section 2079 et seq "to disclose all facts known to the agent materially affecting the value or desirability of the property that are not known to, or within the diligent attention and observation of, the parties. An agent is not obligated to reveal to either party any confidential information obtained from the other party that does not involve the affirmative duties set forth above." (Civ. Code sec. 2079.16.) Courts have found disclosure duties for various conditions. Shapiro v. Sutherland (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1534 held the prior owners had a duty to disclose the material fact of a history of noise complaints against the neighboring property, as they were personally aware of those facts.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DUANE LEE FRISBIE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Here, there is not a sufficient showing that the Settlement Agreement's terms rise to the level of "material" facts or information requiring disclosure. Decedent, as plaintiff in the civil action, filed a Request for Dismissal with prejudice on April 20, 2020, following the arbitration ruling. Facially, the terms of the Settlement Agreement are a private contract between the parties, resolving the litigation. Neither party specifically addressed disclosing the Final Award on Phase 1 Issues attached as Exhibit 1 to the Settlement Agreement to potential buyers and the probate referee as a separate confidential document. There is not a sufficient showing either the Settlement Agreement or the arbitrator's Final Award on Phase 1 Issues contain "material" information impacting the value or desirability of the estate real property. The fact that there has been litigation concerning ownership interests in this tenancy in common is likely within the seller's duty of disclosure to buyers, under both statute and case law. But, on the record before the court, the existence of the prior litigation can be disclosed without disclosing the terms of the Settlement Agreement or the arbitrator's determination and orders. The Motion's request for an order authorizing the Executor's disclosure of the Settlement Agreement document to prospective buyers and the probate referee is DENIED. The April 26, 2004 Tenancy-In-Common Ownership Agreement as amended on December 4 and December 27, 2004, although attached as an exhibit to the Settlement Agreement and referenced in the terms, is both "material" information impacting the estate real property's value or desirability, thus requiring disclosure to buyers, and is now also on the public record as an attachment to the Response to the Motion. As this information may reasonably impact the property's value, it should also be made available to the probate referee to appraise for the purposes of this probate administration. The Motion's request for an order authorizing the Executor to disclose the April 26, 2004 Tenancy-In-Common Ownership Agreement as amended on December 4 and December 27, 2004 to potential buyers and the probate referee is GRANTED. Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
